title: Spray foam insulation: what to know before you buy
subtitle: Mortgage risk, cavity fill concerns, and when to avoid it
excerpt: Spray polyurethane foam is sold aggressively for loft, cavity, and underfloor applications, but it carries mortgage risks and removal complications that make it unsuitable for most UK retrofit applications. This is the decision framework.
sourceNotes: RICS guidance on spray foam and mortgage valuations (2023). gov.uk Consumer Protection guidance on doorstep selling of insulation. Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors factsheet on spray foam in residential properties.

glossaryTerms.definition: A membrane that slows the movement of water vapour through a building element. Required on the warm side of insulation in many assemblies to prevent interstitial condensation.

content.text: Spray polyurethane foam is a two-part mixture that expands on contact with air and sets to a rigid foam structure. Applied at depth it provides good thermal performance. The problem is what happens to the building around it. In UK lofts, cavities, and underfloor spaces, the foam bonds to timber and masonry and blocks access for inspection, wiring, and damp-proofing. The effects on property values and mortgage eligibility are now well-documented.

Most UK mortgage lenders flag properties with spray foam insulation in the loft as non-standard construction. RICS issued guidance in 2023 confirming that spray foam applied to roof timbers makes the structural integrity of the roof impossible to assess visually or physically without removal. A surveyor cannot confirm the condition of the rafters, the ridge board, or the wall plate. This causes valuers to flag the property as a reduced risk, which limits the mortgage products available and reduces the achievable sale price. Several lenders decline to lend on properties with spray foam loft insulation entirely.

Spray foam bonds to timber, tile battens, rafters, and joists. Removal requires either scraping and chemical dissolution (which risks damaging the timber surface) or replacing the bonded timber entirely. In a loft, this means stripping the roof covering, replacing the battens and possibly the rafters, and relaying the roof. The cost of spray foam removal typically runs to several thousand pounds, often exceeding the value of the insulation that was installed.

Injecting spray foam into a cavity wall blocks all future access to the cavity. If the foam bridges from the outer to the inner leaf it creates a moisture path. In exposed locations (coastal, hillside, or west-facing elevations) this is a serious risk. The cavity exists partly to drain water that gets through the outer leaf. Blocking it can cause damp in the inner leaf. This matters most in pre-1930 brick buildings where the cavity is narrow and the mortar joints are permeable. In these cases a proper cavity fill includes a
When spray foam is used legitimately

Expanding foam in a trigger can is a valid sealant for small gaps around pipe penetrations, window frames, and structural joints. This is a different product from the bulk spray-foam systems sold door-to-door for lofts and cavities. Using a small can to seal a 20 mm gap around a pipe is fine. The problems above apply to bulk foam applied to structural timber and masonry.
Loft insulation: mineral wool rolls or blown loose fill between and across joists. Removable, inspectable, mortgage-neutral, and less expensive per m2 than spray foam.
Cavity fill: blown mineral wool bead or polystyrene bead, installed by an MCS-registered installer. Removable in principle (requires filling and redrilling), does not bond to masonry.
Underfloor insulation: mineral wool batts suspended between joists from below, held by netting or timber slats. Removable, does not affect the timber.

Cold-calling and doorstep selling of spray foam insulation is regulated under the Consumer Contracts Regulations 2013. You have 14 days to cancel a contract formed at your home or over the phone. Trading Standards has taken action against spray foam companies for misleading claims about energy savings and mortgage neutrality. If a contractor claims that spray foam will not affect your mortgage, that claim is incorrect for the majority of UK mortgage products.
